coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for femtosecond ultrafast pulsed fiber lasers
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yichao
Ding, Guanyun
Kai, Dongyun
Xie, Xuhuan
Zhang, Fangteng
Zeng, Youjun
Liu, Guanyu
Wu, Haoyi
Nie, Zhaogang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are a new class of quantum dot with unique electronic properties, whose electrons transport and biochemical properties have been extensively studied. However, the optical properties of N-GQDs, especially their nonlinear optical response and applications in ultrafast photonics and photoelectronic, need to be further explored. The nonlinear optical response and ultrafast carrier dynamics of N-GQDs in an aqueous solution are systematically investigated in this work. N-GQDs have an ultrafast carrier cooling delay of about 143 fs in the visible range. In the study of the nonlinear optical response of N-GQDs, the saturable absorption is transformed into reverse saturable absorption as the incident pulse intensity increases, which makes N-GQDs an effective saturable absorber for ultrashort pulse generation in mode-locked Er-doped fiber lasers. Our results may lead to further investigations into the optical properties of N-GQDs and demonstrate potential opportunities for ultrafast photonic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped Graphene quantum dots
Nonlinear optics
Carrier dynamics
Mode-locked
Er-doped fiber laser

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the atomic structure of N-GQDs. (b) TEM image of N-GQDs. (c) XPS spectra of N-GQDs and high-resolution peaks of C1s (d), N1s (e), and O1s (f). (g) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs. (h) The spectrum of UV–Vis-NIR. (i) PL emission spectra (excited under 325 nm).
(a) The transient absorption 2D map of N-GQDs. (b)TA spectra of GQDs between different delay times. (c) Kinetics at the probe wavelength of 553 nm.
(a) Experiment device for non-linear measurement of N-GQDs. (b) Measured saturable absorption and fit of N-GQDs.
Configuration of the oscillator; LD: laser diode; PC: polarization controller; WDM: wavelength division multiplexer; EDF: erbium-doped fiber; OC: optical coupler.

Typical characteristics of pulses output from the fiber laser based on N-GQDs water solution mode-locking. (a) Mode-locked optical spectrum centered at 1559 nm. The bandwidth at 3 dB is 1.92 nm. (b) Typical oscilloscope pulse trains of mode locking; inset: the picture of pulse trains. (c) Radio frequency (RF) spectrum. (d) Autocorrelation trace after a sech2 fitting.
(a) Long-term stability of the mode-locked output pulse, repeated optical spectra measured every 1 h. (b) The drift of the central wavelength and the spectral bandwidth with respect to time.
Comparison of output performance of mode-locking fiber lasers based on various QD materials.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for femtosecond ultrafast pulsed fiber lasers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics & Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510650, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics & Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510650, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Information Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Information Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are a new class of quantum dot with unique electronic properties, whose electrons transport and biochemical properties have been extensively studied. However, the optical properties of N-GQDs, especially their nonlinear optical response and applications in ultrafast photonics and photoelectronic, need to be further explored. The nonlinear optical response and ultrafast carrier dynamics of N-GQDs in an aqueous solution are systematically investigated in this work. N-GQDs have an ultrafast carrier cooling delay of about 143 fs in the visible range. In the study of the nonlinear optical response of N-GQDs, the saturable absorption is transformed into reverse saturable absorption as the incident pulse intensity increases, which makes N-GQDs an effective saturable absorber for ultrashort pulse generation in mode-locked Er-doped fiber lasers. Our results may lead to further investigations into the optical properties of N-GQDs and demonstrate potential opportunities for ultrafast photonic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a member of nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) refer to zero-dimensional graphene materials with a spatial scale of nanometric, and have been the most popular new carbon nanomaterials recently. GQDs have unique physical properties [1–4] and have been applied to bio-imaging [5–7], sensing [8–13], energy storages [14–16], water treatments [17–20] and so on. Doping appropriate elements in graphene quantum dots can modulate the band gap of quantum confinement and edge effects and change the electronic, local chemical, and optical properties of doped graphene quantum dots. Currently, various elements, such as nitrogen [21], boron [13], selenium [22], and fluorine [23], have been doped into graphene quantum dots. Nitrogen has an atomic radius similar to carbon (C:70 pm, N:56 pm), and has five valence electrons that can bond with carbon atoms to form a variety of groups. Therefore, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots are the most widely studied graphene quantum dot materials.

Many quantum dot materials, including BP quantum dots, MXenes quantum dots, and excessive metallic chalcogenide quantum dots, exhibit not only unique electronic properties but also excellent optical response performance due to their unique electron band structure. For example, the electronic-optical properties of BP quantum dots are anomalously size-dependent, and the band gap increases with decreasing volume (0.3 eV-2.0 eV). In the optical response, BP quantum dots have tunable wide-band nonlinear optical properties and exhibit nonlinear absorption in the visible range and saturable absorption in the near-infrared range under ultra-fast pumping excitation [24,25]. Due to the D-shell electrons from the M atom, the Mxenes quantum dots show high electron density close to the Fermi level, showing good metal conductivity. The optical response also shows interesting phenomena such as universal absorption constant, tunable band transition, and excellent saturation absorption characteristics. The results show that the transition metal chalcogenide quantum dots can also be applied to photonics and optoelectronics due to their excellent nonlinear optical properties [26–28]. In the past few years, these quantum dot materials with unique electronic properties and optical responses have been widely used in a variety of optoelectronic devices [29–31]. In contrast, the energy level structure of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots is more complex due to the abundant functional groups around the edge of the structure [32,33], coupled with the large π–conjugated system on N-GQDs, and the electron effect caused by nitrogen doping [18], have not only higher charge transfer efficiency [34] but also exhibit strong two-photon absorption effect [35]. Can these properties lead to surprising discoveries and applications in N-GQDs? Can N-GQDs become an emerging ultrafast photonics material? A large number of previous studies have focused on photoelectric sensing [10,36], fluorescence quenching [11,37], photocatalysis [38,39], and other directions of N-GQDs. However, the nonlinear optical respons, ultrafast carrier dynamics, and their photonic applications of N-GQDs have never been explored, which largely leaves behind the research on their electronic properties.

In this work, we investigated the nonlinear optical response and ultrafast carriers dynamics of N-GQDs, and the ultrafast photonic applications in an Er-doped fiber laser as a saturable absorber are demonstrated. Ultrafast femtosecond transient absorption spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution show that the ultrafast carrier cooling delay time in the visible range is comparable to the graphene time scale. In the study of the nonlinear optical response of N-GQDs, it is found that the N-GQDs-SA changes from saturable absorption to reverse saturable absorption as the incident pulse intensity increases, revealing the possibility that N-GQDS-SA obtains high-energy dissipative soliton pulses. Picosecond pulses can be generated by an Er-doped fiber laser with N-GQDs aqueous solution saturable absorber. Our work suggests that N-GQDs afford new opportunities for photonic applications.

The N-GQDs were synthesized using a low temperature carbonization of ammonium citric acid. Typically, about 2.0 g ammonium citrate tribasic was poured into a corundum crucible and then heated to 180℃ for four hours. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, a black network-like production were obtained and ground into powder. After that, 8.0 mg production was dispersed into 10 mL DI water to obtain a N-GQDs suspension.

The morphology of the sample was investigated using a high-resolutiontransmission electron microscopy(TEM) from FEI Talos F200S. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the sample was studied using a Thermo Fisher Escalab 250Xi equipment. The surface functional groups were determined using a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR) from Thermofisher Nicolet IS50. The photoluminescence (PL) of the sample was performed using a Micro Confocal Raman Spectrometer from LabRAM HR Evolution.

As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the thermal decomposition of ammonium citric acid allows the formation of aromatic rings who crosslink together to construct a graphenen oxide structure. This graphene oxide expands the dimension within 2–4 nm (Fig. 1 (b)), confirming to the quantum size of the materials. The elements mainly constructed the quantum dots are carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which is confirmed by the survey XPS. The deconvolution of the C 1 s peaks suggests that four kind of the chemical bonds associated with the carbon. The sp2/sp3 from the C = C/C-C bonds at 284.8 eV suggests graphite-like backbone structure of the N-GQDs, indicating the appearance of π states along the vertical-align of the backbone. The peaks at 286.4 and 288.5 eV are ascribed to the C = O and O = C-O/C-O bonds on the surface of the N-GQDs, corresponding to the surface –OH and –COOH groups, which are confirmed by O 1 s XPS (Fig. 1 (f)) and the C = O, C-O and OH vibration modes at 1714, 1190 and 3215 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum. The confinement of the size of N-GQDs will induce the π-n transition, corresponding to the optical absorption band at 350 nm π*-n transition in Fig. 1(h). Once the π*-n transition is induced, the return of the excited electron generates a photon emission at about 430 nm (Fig. 1 (i)). The C 1 s XPS peak at 285.1 eV indicates the dopant of nitrogen in the N-GQDs. The formation of the nitrogen including the pyrrolic-, graphitic-, and oxide-N shows some improvement to the PL efficiency. The XRD of the N-GQDs used in this work have been characterised in our previous work [40]. The N-GQDs exhibited the broad diffraction peaks centering at 16 and 27°, corresponding to the (001) and (002) planes' diffraction, respectively.

As an effective technique for investing carrier dynamics, transient absorption (TA) spectra can exhibit the relevant information of carrier thermal dynamics. Here, the carrier dynamics of N-GQDs water solution at room temperature are investigated by ultrafast pump–probe experiments. The TA experiments were performed with narrowband pump at 400 nm and broadband (350–760 nm) probe pulses. Fig. 2 (a) shows the transient absorption 2D map of the N-GQDs water solution. The Y-axis represents the pump–probe delay time in terms of picosecond, and the X-axis represents the probe light wavelength ranging from 430 to 760 nm, indicating that N-GQDs have a broad transient absorption. Fig. 2(b) shows the TA spectra of N-GQDs at several pump–probe delays. There are three kinds of spectral features in the TA spectrum. After the pump light excitation, due to the Pauli exclusion principle, the filling of quantized electronic states will lead to the bleaching of the corresponding optical transitions from the ground state to an excited state, which is named ground state bleaching (GSB). On the other hand, the photoexcited electrons in the excited states could further absorb probe light to higher levels or return to the ground state by stimulated radiation due to the disturbance of probe light, so-called excited-state absorption (ESA) and stimulated emission (SE), respectively. At early time of ∼ 0.5 ps, the TA spectrum shows an obvious difference compared with other spectra, which is reflected in the more prominent SE. This feature is usually associated with the multitude of ultrafast processes – carrier-carrier scattering, carrier-phonon scattering, band-gap renormalization, and band filling – that are simultaneously operative following the pump excitation at short time delays, resulting in the complex TA spectra. The decomposition of the spectral features are beyond the scope of this study. The appearance of oscillations at the long wavelength of the kinetic curve (>620 nm) is due to fluctuations in the probe light after broadening, but it has no effect on the kinetic signal we have extracted at 553 nm, which corresponds to the excited state absorption signal [41]. Fig. 2(c) shows the carrier kinetic curves obtained from the probe light measurement at 553 nm. The solid fitting line is obtained from the convolution of the biexponential decay functions with the instrument response function. The latter dictates the time resolution of the experimental apparatus, determined to be 80 fs from the pump–probe cross-correlation. The fast carrier fast relaxation time τ of N-GQDs (∼143 fs, see Fig. 2(c)) is in the time scale of about 100–200 fs, which is associated with intraband carrier-carrier scattering. The slower response is about 10–20 ps, which is much longer than the intial relaxaion time and could be attributed to optical phonon cooling and interband carrier recombination. The experimental fitting results indicate that the carrier relaxation time τ of N-GQDs is on the same scale as that in graphene [42,43].

The tapered area of a tapered fiber was immersed in a N-GQDs water solution in a sealed glass box as a saturable absorber (SA), as shown in the inset of Fig. 3 (a). Fig. 3(a) schematically illustrates the nonlinear absorption measurement setup. A stable and standard soliton mode-locking fiber laser with an output pulse width of 151 fs and repetition rate of 47.6 MHz was used as the input pulse source. A coupler split the output pulse into two patchcords with the same length. To reduce the errors caused by input power fluctuation, the output powers (with or without passing through the N-GQDs-SA) were detected simultaneously through two sets of power meters with equal optoelectronic responses and parameter setups, including sweeping time. Therefore, the difference of output power from two power meters gives the transmission loss due to the SA of the sample. The nonlinear transmission of the SA device is shown in Fig. 3(b). With increasing the peak power intensity of the pulses, the transmission of the N-GQDs-SA firstly increases and then tends to be constant, which results from saturable absorption. The measured modulation depth is 0.28%. Then it clearly illustrates that the nonlinear behavior alters from the saturable absorption (SA) to reverse saturable absorption (RSA) as the peak power intensity of pulses continues to increase. The transformation from SA to RSA suggests that another nonlinear process takes place and becomes dominant. The first half of the nonlinear transmission curve, indicative of saturable absorption, is apparent. The following formula was used to fit the experimental date:
T
I
= 1 - Δ T exp
- I /
I
Sat
-
T
ns
where
T ( I )
is the transmission,
Δ T
is the modulation depth (maximum value of the transmission shifted to 100% without normalization),
I
is the power of the input light,
I
s a t
is the saturation power, and
T
n s
is the non-saturated loss.

The occurrence of nonlinear curves measured by N-GQDs can be analyzed theoretically by the ground state single-photon absorption induced excited state two-photon absorption model: when the light intensity is small, the electron population is in the ground state, at this point, the electron transfer is mainly between the ground state and first excited state, this medium under strong light absorption is mainly characterized by saturated absorption. The increase of light intensity increases the first excited state of particle number density gradually, and the two-photon absorption performance is more and more apparent. When the two-photon absorption cross section is extensive, and the light intensity is large enough, the medium exhibits reverse saturable absorption characteristics [44]. For conventional solitons (CS), the RSA effect restricts the maximum pulse energy, but has little influence on the soliton shape. For dissipative solitons (DS), the RSA effect increases the maximum pulse energy and plays a key role in transforming the pulse states, which further facilitates the generation of dissipative soliton resonance (DSR) pulses. In normal dispersion regime, RSA contribute to the emergence of DSR pulses [45,46]. When the pump power reaches the reverse saturable absorption region, the transmittance reduction caused by the reverse saturable absorption effect is flattened in the center of the pulse with higher intensity. However, the pulse wings are still in the saturable absorption range due to the low intensity, which will narrow the pulse. Thus, a DSR pulse with a square-wave shape will be obtained [47,48].

Fig. 4 shows the Erbium-doped fiber laser (EDFL) with an N-GQDs-SA. A 1 m long Er-doped fiber (EDF, I-25(980/125), Fibercore Ltd.) was utilized as the gain fiber with the group velocity dispersion (GVD) of 86 ps2/km. The SMF(SMF-28e, Corning Inc.) has a total length of ∼ 50 m in the laser cavity with a GVD of − 23.34 ps2/km. The total length and net dispersion of the cavity are ∼ 51 m and ∼ -1.08 ps2 respectively. The EDF is pumped by a 980 nm laser diode (LD) with a maximum power of 600 mW. A 980/1550 nm pump wavelength division multiplexer (WDM) was employed to couple pump light into the fiber cavity. The polarization-independent isolator (PI-ISO) is used to enhance the unidirectional operation of the laser, which guarantees the unidirectional propagation of the pulses in the cavity and eliminates the reflected light at the end, thus improving the signal-to-noise ratio of the laser pulse. The cavity birefringence and the cavity polarization state are adjusted by the polarization controller (PC). N-GQDs solution drops surround the tapered fiber's conical position to form the SA device. N-GQDS-SA is spliced between PC and optical coupler (OC). The OC is used with an output ratio of 10% for both spectral and temporal diagnostics.

Self-started mode locking occurred at an incident pump power of about 135 mW. The average output power of the laser is 0.752 mW under 135 mW pump power, and the energy conversion efficiency is 3.2%. Fig. 5 (a) shows the optical spectrum of the mode-locked pulses, with a central wavelength at 1559 nm and a 3 dB spectral bandwidth of 1.92 nm. Due to the laser cavity operating in the anomalous dispersion region, symmetrical Kelly-sidebands are observed in the spectra, indicating that there is strong resonant coupling between the soliton and dispersive wave, which results from the periodic perturbations caused by the discrete nature of the loss, dispersion, and nonlinearity devices in this proposed cavity. The output pulse train measured by an oscilloscope is shown in Fig. 5(b). The output pulse period is about 252 ns, corresponding to a cavity length of 51.8 m. The output pulse shape is asymmetrical; the effect of RSA on reducing the trailing edge of the pulse is less than that of SA on reducing the leading edge of the pulse [49]. The RF spectrum has a fundamental repetition rate at 3.964 MHz in Fig. 5(c), which indicates that the mode-locked operation shows a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of about 60 dB. The autocorrelation trace after a sech2 fitting is shown in Fig. 5(d), demonstrating that the pulse duration is 1.36 ps. The time-bandwidth product (TBP) is calculated to be 0.322, slightly larger than the theoretical minimum time-bandwidth product of hyperbolic secant pulse (0.315), indicating that the mode-locked pulses were weakly chirped. It is noteworthy that the performance of the N-GQDs-based mode-locked fiber laser is comparable to those previous works with other 0D materials, as shown in Table. 1 .

To further explore the long-term stability of the output performance of the mode-locked laser, we kept the laser operating at room temperature and air for 8 h continuously and collected the output spectrum every hour (Fig. 6 (a)). The statistical collection results (Fig. 6(b)) showed that the central wavelength of the output spectrum of the laser varies from 1559.08 nm to 1559.28 nm. The bandwidth of 3 dB varies from 1.88 nm to 2.02 nm, all of which are within the error range, indicating that the laser has good long-term working stability.

In summary, we have investigated the optical properties of N-GQDs aqueous solution, especially the nonlinear optical properties and carrier dynamics, and demonstrated its application for ultrafast photonics. The carrier dynamics of N-GQDs are analyzed by TA spectroscopy, and the ultrafast dynamic time is 143 fs. The N-GQDs-SA changes from saturable absorption to reverse saturable absorption with increased incident pulse power. In addition, we demonstrated that the N-GQDs solution is an effective SA for a mode-locked fiber laser. Picosecond pulses were generated from an Er-doped fiber laser based on N-GQDs mode-locking. Our experiment shows that N-GQDs is an excellent ultrafast photonic material and demonstrates its great potential for ultrafast photonic and photoelectronic applications.

Yichao Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Guanyun Ding: Investigation, Data curation. Dongyun Kai: Investigation, Validation. Xuhuan Xie: Investigation, Methodology. Fangteng Zhang: Methodology, Data curation. Youjun Zeng: Methodology, Data curation. Guanyu Liu: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Haoyi Wu: Resources, Formal analysis. Zhaogang Nie: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 12204116 and 11774071) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Pronvince (Grant No. 2021A1515012049).
